stem: An insurer is pricing a flood insurance policy for residential property in a coastal city. Historical data suggests the annual frequency of flood events follows a Poisson distribution with rate λ estimated between 1.98 and 3.49 events/year. Each event causes a loss estimated between $138K and $237K (with the average loss being uncertain due to limited historical claims data). The insurer uses a risk loading factor of 20% above the expected loss. What is the fair premium, and what range of premiums is justified given the uncertainty in both frequency and severity?

ground_truth.answer: Expected loss = λ × mean severity. Range: $273.2K to $827.1K. Point estimate: $515.1K. With 20% loading: premium range [$327.8K, $992.5K]. Point estimate premium: $618.1K.

solution_steps: Expected loss = E[N] × E[X] where N ~ Poisson(λ), X = loss per event.
Note: this assumes independence between frequency and severity. In practice, high-frequency years may also have higher severity (correlation), which would widen the range further.
common_failure_modes: Using only the midpoint estimates without computing the full range
Adding frequency and severity instead of multiplying
Not including the risk loading in the premium calculation
Treating the expected loss as the premium without risk margin
Assuming frequency and severity are independent when they may be correlated
